raw_case:
Metastatic Follicular Carcinoma of the Thyroid: A Case Report
Neck lumps may indicate metabolic disorders of the parathyroid and thyroid glands commonly present in the anterior aspect of the neck. Some neck lumps are detected as follicular thyroid cancer. Follicular thyroid cancer is a malignant epithelial tumor that shows evidence of follicular cell differentiation but lacks the characteristic nuclear features of papillary thyroid carcinoma (PTC). Iodine-deficient regions have higher rates of follicular carcinoma. There has been a decline in the incidence of this kind of tumor in recent years. As with PTC, prior radiation therapy increases the risk of follicular cancer, but to a lesser extent. In some cases, patients have distant metastases that involve the bones. Follicular cancer is divided into minimally invasive and widely invasive types. Early detection is important. Treatment usually consists of thyroidectomy and radioactive iodine therapy, and hormone replacement therapy may be necessary. Fine needle aspiration cytology is an efficient and cost-effective tool for the diagnosis of neck swelling and has the potential to diagnose the mass. We report the case of a 60-year-old Indian woman who had been experiencing neck swelling for the last 12 years. The neglected neck mass was confirmed as a hyperechoic mass with microcalcifications on ultrasound, representative of metastatic follicular thyroid carcinoma, which further spread to the ribs and the vertebrae. The patient was followed without complications.

Introduction

The function of the thyroid gland is to produce thyroid hormones that the body needs to perform various metabolic functions. Follicles make up the thyroid gland and are the functional and structural units of the thyroid gland. These cells can grow abnormally and lead to serious follicular disease. One of the most prevalent endocrine tumors, thyroid cancer can be classified as differentiated or undifferentiated [1]. Atypical cancers include those that are papillary and follicular, and typical types include those that are medullary and anaplastic. Follicular thyroid cancer is the second most common type and accounts for 10-15% of all thyroid cancers [2]. Up to half of follicular cancer cases show RAS mutations, one-third show PAX-PPARG mutations, and only 3% show both [2,3]. Ultrasound imaging (USG) is the primary modality of choice, which shows hypoechoic soft tissue nodules with ill-defined borders, microcalcifications, and a tubular pattern with abundant internal flow, unlike the surrounding nodules. Computed tomography (CT) and USG-guided fine-needle aspiration (FNA) or core biopsy can be utilized to diagnose primary thyroid cancer quickly [3]. Early diagnosis reduces delay in treatment and prognosis gets better.

Case presentation

A 60-year-old Indian female presented with swelling across her anterior neck region for 12 years, which was insidious onset initially pea-sized. Painless progression of the swelling was noticed in the last two years. The patient also complained of chest wall pain and swelling. It was not associated with dyspnea, hand tremors, breathlessness, voice change, palpitation, or any co-morbidities. Clinically, the swelling was solitary and firm in consistency on the anterior aspect of the neck, measuring 12 x 8 cm in size (Figure 1).The swelling involved both lobes of the thyroid gland and the isthmus, along with evidence of engorged veins. Retrosternal extension of the gland noted. The patient had no palpable axillary lymph nodes. The patient's thyroid profile was suggestive of euthyroidism with thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH) (0.746 μIU/ml), FT3 (2.77 pg/ml), and FT4 (0.78 ng/dl) values. The anterior view of the neck revealed a well-defined swelling over the anterior aspect of the neck, more toward the right. There was evidence of a mass effect in the form of slight tracheal deviation. On the anteroposterior view, it was suggestive of tracheal deviation toward the right side (Figure 2).Chest radiograph revealed left-sided pleural-based mass extending along the fifth, sixth, and seventh ribs (Figure 3).USG of the neck revealed an anechoic cystic lesion with multiple septations (Figure 4) within and an isoechoic lesion in the right lobe. A well-defined isoechoic lesion with peripheral calcification was also seen in the right lobe. Isthmus appeared bulky with a heterogenous echo texture. The left lobe of the thyroid appeared bulky, heterogeneous in texture, and multiloculated causing mass effect.Non-contrast CT scan was suggestive of an enlarged thyroid gland (Figure 5). Contrast-enhanced CT (CECT) of the neck and thorax revealed large heterogeneously enhancing soft tissue density arising from the left lobe of the thyroid, likely of neoplastic etiology. Heterogeneously enhancing soft tissue density from the fifth rib and lytic lesion in the body of the D12 vertebra were suggestive of metastasis (Figures 6-10).On fine-needle aspiration cytology (FNAC) of the fifth rib, there was evidence of hyperchromic nuclei with mild pleomorphism, prominent micronuclei, and uneven chromatin, suggesting deposits of follicular thyroid carcinoma. The patient was subjected to a debulking surgery: total thyroidectomy with a modified radical neck dissection. The excised sample (Figure 11) was subjected to histopathological analysis which confirmed the diagnosis of follicular thyroid carcinoma (Figure 12). The patient was planned for palliative radiotherapy followed by chemotherapy. Postoperatively, tab levothyroxine (100 μg) was started. At the three-month follow-up, the patient had no fresh complaints, the scar site was noted to be healthy, and the metastatic workup showed no other significant metastasis. 

Discussion

A neck lump may be a symptom of thyroid and parathyroid gland metabolic diseases, though there are a few inactive lesions too. Asymptomatic individuals might also develop a clinical presentation in the form of goiter. Thyroid cancer is the most frequent endocrine malignancy. Thyroid malignancy is common in females, constitutes nearly 3% of all cancers, and has been reported with a trend of increased incidence in the last few decades [2,4].Follicular thyroid carcinoma, which makes up 15% of thyroid cancer cases, is more frequent in women over 50 years of age [3]. The current case presented with an enlarged 12 x 8 cm in the neck, which could be a result of neglected goiter over a period of time. Swelling of the neck might have underlying reasons such as lymphadenopathy and metastatic carcinomas [3,4]. Differential diagnoses can be concluded as papillary thyroid carcinoma, follicular adenoma, noninvasive follicular thyroid neoplasm with papillary-like nuclear features, poorly differentiated thyroid carcinoma, and metastatic non-thyroid carcinomas. FNAC continues to be a trustworthy investigation for head and neck swellings in the neck and the head. A study on neck swelling reported FNAC as an efficient and cost-effective tool with nearly no risk and minimum complications, leading to a conclusive diagnosis of the superficial mass [3,5,6]. The study reported the diagnostic accuracy of FNAC for these cancers, with a few cases as inconclusive. Overall diagnostic accuracy of the FNAC for thyroid cancers is reported as 85.14% [5]. Similarly, the FNAC report of this patient concluded follicular thyroid carcinoma and was further subject to its management.Surgery is the most preferred treatment along with adjuvant therapy with radioactive iodine and follow-up to keep a check on the disease recurrence. Radioactive iodine (RAI) instead of chemotherapy is the treatment with debunking. RAI low doses for differentiated thyroid cancer have good survival rates [7,8]. For a patient with neck swelling/ thyroid swelling, the approach would be USG or CT scan followed by tissue diagnosis with the help of FNAC for confirmation of the diagnosis. Timely diagnosis and intervention are recommended in neck masses with a gradual increase in size, which might be helpful in the prevention of adverse outcomes.

Conclusions

Any neck mass that is progressive in nature should not be ignored, as it might be the result of certain lymphadenopathy or malignancy, which might cause adverse outcomes if not timely addressed. Regular follow-up is important to monitor for any recurrence or spread. Overall, the prognosis of follicular carcinoma is generally favorable, with a good success rate of the treatment and long-term survival.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: 60-year-old Indian female
- Chief Complaint: Swelling in the anterior neck for the past 12 years.
- History of Present Illness: Gradual swelling in the anterior neck, initially pea-sized, painless progression noticed in the last two years. Associated new complaint of chest wall pain and swelling. No complaints of dyspnea, hand tremors, breathlessness, voice change, or palpitations. 
- Past Medical History: None reported.
- Personal History: None reported.
- Family History: None reported.
- Physical Examination: Solitary, firm, 12 x 8 cm swelling involving both thyroid lobes and the isthmus. Mass effect observed with slight tracheal deviation to the right. Evidence of engorged veins and retrosternal extension; no palpable axillary lymph nodes.
- Ancillary Tests: 
  - Thyroid profile: Euthyroid (TSH: 0.746 μIU/ml, FT3: 2.77 pg/ml, FT4: 0.78 ng/dl).
  - Chest radiograph: Left-sided pleural-based mass involving the 5th, 6th, and 7th ribs.
  - Ultrasound (USG): Anechoic cystic lesion with septations in the neck; isoechoic lesions with calcifications in the right thyroid lobe; left thyroid lobe heterogeneous and bulky with multiloculated texture causing a mass effect.
  - CT (both non-contrast and contrast-enhanced): Enlarged thyroid gland with heterogeneously enhancing soft-tissue densities in the left thyroid lobe. Lytic lesion in the D12 vertebra; rib lesions consistent with metastasis.
  - Fine-Needle Aspiration Cytology (FNAC): Follicular thyroid carcinoma with hyperchromic nuclei, mild pleomorphism, and uneven chromatin.
  - Histopathology: Confirmed follicular thyroid carcinoma following total thyroidectomy and modified radical neck dissection.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- **Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma:** Excluded as the mass lacked characteristic nuclear features (e.g., Orphan Annie nuclei) and FNAC confirmed follicular tumor characteristics. 
- **Follicular Adenoma:** Excluded due to invasive metastatic manifestations (rib and vertebral lesions) present in this case, which are absent in adenomas.
- **Noninvasive Follicular Thyroid Neoplasm with Papillary-Like Nuclear Features (NIFTP):** Excluded due to invasive features and metastasis, which classify it as a carcinoma.
- **Poorly Differentiated Thyroid Carcinoma:** Excluded since FNAC and histopathology findings explicitly confirmed follicular differentiation without more dedifferentiated features.
- **Metastatic Non-Thyroid Carcinomas:** Excluded as imaging and FNAC confirmed origin from the thyroid gland.
- **Final Diagnosis Pathway:** FNAC initially suggested follicular thyroid carcinoma with metastatic deposits (rib and spine), and histopathological evaluation post-surgery solidified the diagnosis.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Metastatic Follicular Thyroid Carcinoma with rib and vertebral metastases. Confirmed via FNAC, imaging studies, and histopathology. Patient underwent total thyroidectomy, modified radical neck dissection, and initiated on levothyroxine for post-surgical management. Planned for palliative radiotherapy and chemotherapy.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's start by reviewing the case. The patient is a 60-year-old Indian female with a 12-year history of anterior neck swelling. The swelling started small and painless but has progressed over the last two years. Now she has chest wall pain and swelling. No symptoms like dyspnea, tremors, or voice changes. 

First, the long duration of the neck swelling makes me think of a slow-growing process. Thyroid issues are likely here. The fact that it's painless and progressive points towards a neoplasm, either benign or malignant. Common thyroid nodules can be benign, but the progression and associated chest symptoms raise red flags.

Looking at the physical exam findings: a firm, large thyroid mass involving both lobes and the isthmus. Tracheal deviation suggests a mass effect. Engorged veins and retrosternal extension might indicate compression or invasion. The absence of palpable lymph nodes is interesting because thyroid cancers can spread to lymph nodes, but some types, like follicular, tend to metastasize hematogenously instead.

The thyroid profile is euthyroid, so hyper/hypothyroidism isn't contributing. That's typical in thyroid cancers unless there's a functional tumor, which is rare.

Imaging: The chest X-ray shows a pleural-based mass involving ribs. That's concerning for metastasis. The ultrasound describes cystic and solid components with calcifications. Calcifications can be seen in malignancies, like psammoma bodies in papillary carcinoma, but here the FNAC says follicular. CT shows heterogenous thyroid enlargement and lytic lesions in the vertebra and ribs. Lytic bone lesions are classic for metastatic disease. Follicular thyroid carcinoma is known to spread via blood to bones and lungs.

The FNAC result mentions follicular thyroid carcinoma with hyperchromic nuclei and pleomorphism. Follicular carcinoma is a differentiated thyroid cancer, and diagnosis on cytology can be tricky because it requires seeing capsular or vascular invasion, which FNAC can't assess. But the histopathology after surgery confirmed it, so that's definitive.

Putting it all together: The primary is follicular thyroid carcinoma. The chest wall mass and rib lesions, along with vertebral lytic lesions, are metastases. Follicular carcinoma often metastasizes to bones and lungs, which fits here. The chest pain and swelling are likely due to these metastases. The initial slow growth over 12 years might indicate a well-differentiated tumor that eventually became aggressive enough to metastasize.

Other possibilities? Anaplastic thyroid cancer is more aggressive but usually presents with rapid growth and symptoms. Medullary carcinoma might have different markers, like elevated calcitonin, but the thyroid profile here is normal. The FNAC and histopathology confirm follicular, so that's solid.

Final diagnosis would be metastatic follicular thyroid carcinoma with bone and chest wall metastases. The staging would be advanced, given the distant metastases. The treatment would likely involve total thyroidectomy (which they did), possible radioactive iodine, and management of metastases.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with a long-standing (12-year), painless anterior neck swelling, consistent with a thyroid mass. The recent progression and associated chest wall pain/swelling raise suspicion for malignancy with potential metastasis.

<step 2> Physical examination reveals a large, firm thyroid mass causing tracheal deviation, retrosternal extension, and engorged veins. These findings suggest significant mass effect and possible local invasion, which are concerning for malignancy.

<step 3> Imaging (USG, CT) confirms a heterogeneous thyroid lesion with calcifications and mass effect. The lytic vertebral and rib lesions, along with the pleural-based chest mass, strongly indicate metastatic disease.

<step 4> FNAC and histopathology confirm follicular thyroid carcinoma. Follicular carcinoma is known for hematogenous spread to bones and lungs, aligning with the metastatic rib and vertebral lesions observed here.

<step 5> The euthyroid status rules out functional thyroid disorders, and the absence of lymphadenopathy supports follicular carcinoma’s typical pattern of distant metastasis rather than regional lymphatic spread.

### Answer:
Metastatic follicular thyroid carcinoma with bone and chest wall metastases.

reasoning_eval.step: The patient presents with a long-standing (12-year), painless anterior neck swelling, consistent with a thyroid mass. The recent progression and associated chest wall pain/swelling raise suspicion for malignancy with potential metastasis.

Physical examination reveals a large, firm thyroid mass causing tracheal deviation, retrosternal extension, and engorged veins. These findings suggest significant mass effect and possible local invasion, which are concerning for malignancy.

Imaging (USG, CT) confirms a heterogeneous thyroid lesion with calcifications and mass effect. The lytic vertebral and rib lesions, along with the pleural-based chest mass, strongly indicate metastatic disease.

FNAC and histopathology confirm follicular thyroid carcinoma. Follicular carcinoma is known for hematogenous spread to bones and lungs, aligning with the metastatic rib and vertebral lesions observed here.

The euthyroid status rules out functional thyroid disorders, and the absence of lymphadenopathy supports follicular carcinoma’s typical pattern of distant metastasis rather than regional lymphatic spread.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: Excluded Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma as the mass lacked characteristic nuclear features and FNAC confirmed follicular tumor characteristics.
Step 2: Excluded Follicular Adenoma due to invasive metastatic manifestations (rib and vertebral lesions) present in this case, which are absent in adenomas.
Step 3: Excluded Noninvasive Follicular Thyroid Neoplasm with Papillary-Like Nuclear Features (NIFTP) due to invasive features and metastasis, which classify it as a carcinoma.
Step 4: Excluded Poorly Differentiated Thyroid Carcinoma as FNAC and histopathology findings explicitly confirmed follicular differentiation without more dedifferentiated features.
Step 5: Excluded Metastatic Non-Thyroid Carcinomas as imaging and FNAC confirmed origin from the thyroid gland.
Step 6: FNAC initially suggested follicular thyroid carcinoma with metastatic deposits (rib and spine).
Step 7: Histopathological evaluation post-surgery solidified the diagnosis of metastatic follicular thyroid carcinoma with rib and vertebral metastases.
Step 8: Final diagnosis: Metastatic Follicular Thyroid Carcinoma with rib and vertebral metastases, confirmed via FNAC, imaging studies, and histopathology.
Step 9: Patient underwent total thyroidectomy, modified radical neck dissection, and initiated on levothyroxine for post-surgical management.
Step 10: Planned for palliative radiotherapy and chemotherapy.
